text: Variables, simple data types, print statements, and basic input output in Python.
A Python list stores ordered values and can be changed with append remove and indexing.
If else statements choose different code paths from simple true or false conditions.
For loops repeat an action over a list, string, or range of numbers.
A function groups reusable Python code with parameters and a return value.
HTML headings paragraphs links and images create the basic structure of a web page.
CSS colors fonts margins and padding change how a web page looks.
SQL select from where retrieves rows from a single database table.
A primary key uniquely identifies a row in a simple database table.
Mean median and mode summarize the center of a small dataset.
A variable stores a value that can be used later in a program.
Basic arithmetic operators add subtract multiply divide and calculate remainders.
A web form collects user input and sends it to a server.
A table contains rows and columns for storing structured data.
A probability value describes how likely a simple event is to happen.
Lists tuples dictionaries and sets store collections of values in Python.
A browser sends an HTTP request and receives a response from a web server.
A matrix is a rectangular arrangement of numbers with rows and columns.
Linear equations with one variable can be solved by isolating the unknown.
A basic chart shows values so patterns are easier to see.
Nested loops, file handling, exceptions, and modules require combining several Python concepts.
Object oriented programming uses classes objects methods inheritance and encapsulation.
List comprehensions and lambda functions make data transformations shorter but less obvious.
Database joins combine rows from multiple related tables using primary and foreign keys.
Group by and aggregate functions summarize database records by category.
Normalization reduces duplicated database data by splitting entities into related tables.
REST APIs use endpoints methods JSON request bodies and response status codes.
Authentication and authorization control user identity and permissions in a web app.
Responsive design adapts layouts across mobile tablet and desktop screens.
Linear regression fits a line to predict a continuous target variable.
K nearest neighbor classifies data points using distance to labeled examples.
K means clustering groups unlabeled samples around cluster centroids.
Naive Bayes estimates class probabilities from word frequencies and class priors.
Train test split evaluates whether a model can generalize to unseen examples.
Accuracy precision recall and f1 score describe classification performance.
Confusion matrix analysis shows which classes are commonly mistaken.
Derivatives measure how quickly a function changes at a point.
Integrals calculate accumulated quantity or area under a curve.
Variance and standard deviation measure how spread out data values are.
Feature scaling helps distance based algorithms compare numeric variables fairly.
Regularization, cross validation, and hyperparameter tuning reduce overfitting risk.
Gradient descent optimizes model parameters by following the negative gradient of a loss function.
Multinomial Naive Bayes uses log probabilities and Laplace smoothing to avoid zero likelihoods.
Cosine similarity compares sparse text vectors by their direction rather than their length.
Model serialization stores trained parameters so prediction can run without retraining.
A many to many relationship needs a junction table and careful constraint design.
Transaction isolation levels affect concurrency, locking, and data consistency.
Query optimization uses indexes execution plans and selectivity to reduce database cost.
Backend file upload pipelines must validate file type size temporary storage and cleanup.
Stateful Streamlit applications rerun scripts while preserving selected session variables.
A classifier can overfit when feature space grows quickly with n gram text features.
Cross validation across several folds gives a more stable estimate than one train test split.
Bias variance tradeoff explains how model complexity affects training and test errors.
Partial derivatives and gradients generalize derivative ideas to multivariable functions.
Matrix inverse, determinant, eigenvectors, and vector spaces support linear algebra models.
Bayes theorem computes posterior probability from prior probability and likelihood.
Advanced probability combines conditional probability independence expectation and variance.
Feature engineering for text classification balances vocabulary size against noisy sparse terms.
Production ML systems need monitoring, fallback behavior, and clear separation between training and inference.
Evaluation leakage happens when information from test data influences training decisions.
Beginner lessons introduce one concept at a time with direct examples and simple vocabulary.
A simple Python lesson explains what a variable is and shows assigning a number to a name.
A first programming lesson usually covers print input strings numbers and comments.
A beginner database lesson explains table row column and record with a small example.
A simple web lesson introduces HTML tags such as heading paragraph list and link.
A beginner CSS lesson changes text color background color and font size.
A first math lesson explains addition subtraction multiplication division and remainder.
A simple statistics lesson calculates average value from a short list of numbers.
A beginner probability lesson uses coin toss dice roll and simple event examples.
A basic loop lesson counts from one to ten and prints each value.
A beginner condition lesson compares two numbers and chooses the larger one.
A simple function lesson defines one function and calls it with one argument.
A beginner file lesson reads a short text file line by line.
A basic SQL lesson selects all columns from one table without joins.
A beginner chart lesson displays a bar chart from a few category values.
A simple matrix lesson identifies rows columns and individual elements.
A beginner HTTP lesson explains browser client server request and response.
A basic data lesson separates input process and output in a small program.
A simple quiz lesson asks one direct question and gives one correct answer.
A beginner transcript lesson only identifies main terms without advanced analysis.
Intermediate lessons combine multiple concepts and require comparing related ideas.
An intermediate Python lesson combines functions lists dictionaries and loops in one program.
An intermediate OOP lesson creates multiple classes and explains object relationships.
An intermediate exception lesson catches different error types and handles invalid input.
An intermediate database lesson joins customer order and product tables.
An intermediate SQL lesson uses group by having aggregate functions and order by.
An intermediate web lesson connects a form to a backend API and stores submitted data.
An intermediate REST lesson explains endpoints methods status codes and JSON payloads.
An intermediate ML lesson compares classification and regression with train test split.
An intermediate KNN lesson chooses k and compares Euclidean and cosine distance.
An intermediate Naive Bayes lesson explains prior likelihood posterior and smoothing.
An intermediate clustering lesson explains centroid update and convergence in k means.
An intermediate evaluation lesson reads accuracy precision recall f1 and confusion matrix.
An intermediate calculus lesson computes derivatives and interprets slope.
An intermediate integral lesson solves area under a curve with basic formulas.
An intermediate statistics lesson compares variance standard deviation and distribution.
An intermediate linear algebra lesson multiplies matrices and calculates dot products.
An intermediate feature lesson converts text to bag of words and n gram vectors.
An intermediate deployment lesson runs a local app and configures environment variables.
An intermediate project lesson combines upload processing prediction and export.
Advanced lessons involve abstraction, optimization, tradeoffs, and failure modes.
An advanced Python lesson designs decorators generators context managers and custom iterators.
An advanced architecture lesson separates UI service layer model inference and persistence.
An advanced ML lesson discusses bias variance tradeoff and model generalization limits.
An advanced validation lesson prevents data leakage across preprocessing training and testing.
An advanced text classification lesson analyzes sparse high dimensional vectors and vocabulary growth.
An advanced Bayes lesson derives log likelihood scores and compares numerical stability.
An advanced KNN lesson studies curse of dimensionality and weighted neighbor voting.
An advanced optimization lesson derives gradients and updates parameters using learning rate schedules.
An advanced database lesson analyzes indexes query plans transaction isolation and locking.
An advanced API lesson designs authentication authorization rate limiting and error contracts.
An advanced deployment lesson handles monitoring rollback secrets and production configuration.
An advanced probability lesson proves conditional independence and expectation identities.
An advanced linear algebra lesson connects eigenvalues eigenvectors vector spaces and transformations.
An advanced calculus lesson works with partial derivatives gradients and multivariable functions.
An advanced evaluation lesson compares cross validation macro f1 micro f1 and class imbalance.
An advanced regularization lesson contrasts l1 l2 penalty and overfitting control.
An advanced clustering lesson evaluates clusters using inertia silhouette score and initialization.
An advanced system lesson designs fallback behavior when external AI services fail.
An advanced report lesson explains assumptions limitations reproducibility and experimental risk.
A beginner loop lesson repeats a print statement for each item in a short list.
A beginner for loop lesson uses range to count numbers and display simple output.
A beginner control flow lesson uses if else with one condition and one result.
A beginner database relationship lesson only explains that one table can connect to another.
A beginner classification lesson simply says a model chooses one category from several categories.
A beginner web layout lesson changes width height color and spacing without responsive rules.
A beginner statistics lesson counts correct and incorrect answers without advanced metrics.
A beginner Python lesson explains session variables as values that remember information.
A beginner table lesson describes records columns and simple links between tables.
A beginner machine learning lesson introduces training data and test data with a small example.
An intermediate train test split lesson explains why test data must be separate from training data.
An intermediate model evaluation lesson calculates accuracy from correct and incorrect predictions.
An intermediate confusion matrix lesson compares actual labels with predicted labels for each class.
An intermediate metrics lesson explains precision recall and f1 score without deriving formulas.
An intermediate Naive Bayes lesson counts word frequency for each class and predicts the highest score.
An intermediate Bayes lesson applies prior likelihood and posterior to a text classification example.
An intermediate responsive design lesson uses breakpoints and flexible grids for different screens.
An intermediate frontend lesson adjusts layout for desktop and mobile with media queries.
An intermediate Streamlit lesson uses session state to preserve uploaded file results after rerun.
An intermediate API lesson sends JSON data from frontend to backend and handles simple errors.
An intermediate database design lesson creates foreign keys and explains one to many relationships.
An intermediate many to many lesson introduces a junction table with a student course example.
An intermediate ML lesson compares overfitting and underfitting using train and test accuracy.
An intermediate feature lesson uses bag of words to convert a transcript into numeric counts.
An intermediate quiz lesson validates selected answers and displays a short explanation.
An advanced many to many lesson enforces junction table constraints, composite keys, and cascading rules.
An advanced Streamlit lesson explains rerun behavior, widget identity, cache invalidation, and session state consistency.
An advanced model evaluation lesson analyzes macro average, weighted average, imbalance, and threshold effects.
An advanced Naive Bayes lesson derives log posterior scores and explains independence assumptions.
An advanced responsive design lesson resolves layout shift, overflow, accessibility, and cross browser rendering issues.
An advanced train test lesson discusses leakage, stratification, variance, and reproducibility across random seeds.
An advanced database relationship lesson compares normalization tradeoffs, indexes, and transaction consistency.
An advanced feature engineering lesson reduces sparse noise using vocabulary filtering and n gram selection.
An advanced API lesson handles retry policy, timeout strategy, idempotency, and observability.
An advanced ML system lesson separates offline training, online inference, monitoring, and rollback.
